MARKET.head: Collectible tequila market surges on auction record and investment interest
MARKET.summ: With tequila hitting a new world auction record in recent weeks, and WineFi offering irs first premium tequila investment barrels, is the agave spirit set for a new boom? Arabella Mileham reports
takeaways: Clase Azul Dia de Muertos bottle sold for US$35,000 at Sotheby's, setting new tequila auction record and first top lot for spirits.
Global tequila market valued at US$12.6bn, projected to grow 9% annually through 2033 to reach US$21.6bn.
Younger consumers driving demand for premium and super-premium tequila expressions, similar to whisky 15 years ago.
Supply constraints from regulation, agricultural timelines, and maturation requirements support investment case, with historic returns cited at 15–35%.
Clase Azul's rare 2017 Dia de Muertos release comprised only 300 bottles, originally retailed at US$250 from single Cabo location.

MARKET.head: Breaky Bottom, English wine pioneer, sells for first time in 52 years
MARKET.summ: Breaky Bottom, one of the founding estates of the modern English wine movement, has been put up for sale for the first time since its establishment in 1974. The Sussex vineyard, created by late English wine pioneer Peter Hall, carries a guide price of £4 million
takeaways: Breaky Bottom vineyard founded in 1974 by Peter Hall is on sale for £4 million following Hall's death in October 2025.
The 15-acre Sussex estate produces around 10,000 bottles annually, focusing on sparkling wines from Seyval Blanc and Chardonnay-Pinot blends.
Hall's wines were served at King Charles III's coronation, Queen Elizabeth II's Platinum Jubilee, and the 2012 London Olympics.
English wine production rose 39% in 2025 to 16.5 million bottles equivalent, driving continued investor interest in vineyard assets.
Knight Frank expects international buyers, citing resilient demand for small-scale, terroir-driven producers over mass-market brands.

MARKET.head: Babich Wines marks 110 years as New Zealand's rare family-owned producer
MARKET.summ: From a Croatian immigrant filling barrels by hand to exports in more than 60 countries, Babich Wines' 110-year journey reflects the rise of New Zealand wine itself
takeaways: Founded in 1916 by Croatian immigrant Josip Babich, the winery exports to over 60 countries and remains wholly family-owned across four generations.
American servicemen stationed in New Zealand during World War II purchased Babich wines in U.S. dollars, providing crucial cash flow that eased decades of financial struggle.
Export sales surpassed domestic sales by 1997 after a German merchant's chance encounter sparked overseas expansion; Babich now operates 14 vineyards across 711 hectares.

MARKET.head: Franzia shifts to Australian wine in China to dodge U.S. tariffs
MARKET.summ: Faced with nearly 80% import taxes, one of the world's best-selling boxed wine brands has abandoned U.S.-made wine in China, replacing it with Australian wine bottled locally
takeaways: Nearly 80% total tax burden on U.S. wine imports made Franzia's affordable pricing model unsustainable in China.
Franzia now sources Australian bulk wine, bottled and packaged locally in China instead of importing finished U.S. products.
Products sold in China list China as country of origin with Australia noted only in fine print as base wine source.
Local packaging allows faster response to Chinese market trends, promotions and seasonal campaigns versus U.S. factory constraints.
Brand could resume U.S. imports if tariffs decline, but current tax levels eliminate price competitiveness.

MARKET.head: Japanese wine awards boost awareness but sales impact remains unclear
MARKET.summ: Japanese wines have long been recognised by the likes of DWWA and IWSC, but for a country whose majority of wineries are mom-and-pop shops, do these accolades actually matter to producers looking to grow exports? Joyce Yip finds out
takeaways: Large Japanese producers like Château Mercian win major international awards; small family wineries rarely compete due to export costs and domestic demand.
Katsunumi Jyozo credits early award medals with securing European distribution through Bernard Magrez Group over two decades ago.
Château Mercian attributes growth more to high-profile exposure—such as serving at the 2016 G7 summit—than to awards themselves.
Hong Kong importer Kenneth Lee says awards once mattered more but now compete with abundant wine critics and competitions worldwide.
High production costs from Japan's mountainous terrain and weather challenges make pricing the biggest obstacle to overseas market growth.

CULTURE.head: London masterclass explores rosé diversity beyond pink-wine stereotypes
CULTURE.summ: Think you might be getting stuck in a rosé rut? Now you can find a pink wine that fits your present mood or preferred food at an evening masterclass in London on 10 September
takeaways: Rosé tasting masterclass scheduled for September 10 at The Chelsea Townhouse in London, hosted by Master of Wine Patrick Schmitt.
Event limited to 28 guests and covers varied rosé styles, from niche producers to mainstream brands.
Masterclass aims to help drinkers escape rosé ruts by matching wines to mood and food preferences.
Part of What to Drink series by the drinks business; offers producers direct access to rosé consumers.

CULTURE.head: Pol Roger releases 2019 vintage from exceptional Champagne harvest
CULTURE.summ: Champagne Pol Roger has unveiled its latest vintage expression from the exceptional 2019 harvest, and Patrick Schmitt MW gives his verdict
takeaways: 2019 harvest faced frost, mildew, hail, and heat but yielded wines with rare harmony and freshness.
Pol Roger's 2019 marks first vintage under new cellar master Damien Cambres, replacing Dominique Petit.
Wine contains 63.5% Pinot Noir and 36.5% Chardonnay, aged six years in 33-meter-deep cellars.
Average potential alcohol of 10.7% exceeds 2018's 10.2%, reflecting vintage power and ripeness.
At £365 per six-pack, the 2019 is 10% cheaper than 2018, with ageing potential of 3-5 years.

CULTURE.head: Sam Neill, Two Paddocks founder, dies at 78
CULTURE.summ: Sam Neill, the actor behind Jurassic Park's Dr Alan Grant and founder of Central Otago winery Two Paddocks, has died aged 78. While celebrated across the world for his film career, he spent more than three decades quietly building one of New Zealand's most respected Pinot Noir estates
takeaways: Two Paddocks founder and actor Sam Neill died aged 78 in Sydney after a sudden, unexpected illness.
Neill planted Two Paddocks' first vineyard in Central Otago's Gibbston valley in 1993 and expanded to four sites across three valleys.
The winery became certified organic from 2017 vintage and earned acclaim for restrained, estate-grown Pinot Noir rather than celebrity appeal.
Neill rejected the 'celebrity winemaker' label, arguing it undermined the quality of wines and the work of those producing them.
He championed Burgundian vineyard principles and site-specific New Zealand Pinot Noir during an era when Sauvignon Blanc dominated the country's reputation.

CULTURE.head: Nyetimber wins third title as English wine rankings expand dramatically
CULTURE.summ: Last year Nyetimber topped the charts in this comprehensive blind tasting of English wines carried out by Australian publication The Real Review. But has the Sussex producer managed to hold onto its crown in 2026, or has it been eclipsed by another contender? Anthony Rose reports
takeaways: Nyetimber retains top position in The Real Review's UK rankings, becoming the first three-time winner.
Coates & Seely surges from 12th to 3rd place, marking an unprecedented leap in competition history.
Hampshire emerges as most dynamic region, with multiple producers climbing rankings significantly.
Ten new wineries enter ranked list; total field grows to 39 producers, signaling industry depth and quality spread.
Still wine producers gaining prominence; debate emerging over whether ranking methodology should adjust weighting.

CULTURE.head: Unfiltered wine festival debuts in Berkeley with unconventional producer focus
CULTURE.summ: The two-day event will showcase pours and hot discussion topics from producers who are supported and promoted beyond the festival July 13, 2026 (Berkeley, CA) , Unfiltered: A Celebration of
takeaways: Two-day event scheduled for July 13, 2026 in Berkeley, California.
Festival features wines from producers receiving year-round support beyond the event.
Programming includes tastings alongside discussions of current industry topics.

SCIENCE.head: Grape sunburn spreads north as climate change brings heat waves to new regions
SCIENCE.summ: As climate change makes heat waves more prevalent, more producers have to combat sunburn in grapes, and the flabby, flat, rubbery notes they can bring to wine
takeaways: Direct sun can heat berry surfaces 22-27°F above ambient air temperature; necrosis damage can occur within minutes at extreme temperatures.
Leaf removal timing now critical: defoliation at flowering produces less sunburn than at veraison; mid-summer removal before heat waves significantly increases risk.
Producers in warm regions are shifting away from summer defoliation and adopting kaolin clay sprays and canopy retention to keep fruit shaded.
Sunburned wines show cooked flavors, light color with brown rim, high alcohol, flabby acid, and premature aging; sorting burned fruit remains essential defense.

SCIENCE.head: France's heatwave cuts grape yields across Champagne, Bordeaux, Burgundy
SCIENCE.summ: Weeks of drought are reducing yields and raising quality concerns, though weak global demand means shortages are far from certain
takeaways: Champagne expects grape production to fall around 10% this year; Bordeaux and Burgundy forecast declines of 20% or more.
Extreme heat accelerates ripening, risking higher alcohol levels, lower acidity, and imbalanced wines despite smaller berries.
French regions are harvesting earlier than ever—Champagne potentially in mid-August, a month earlier than historical norms.
Widespread shortages unlikely due to oversupply in entry-level Bordeaux, but premium whites and Sauternes face tighter supplies.
Irrigation now authorized in some regions like Pauillac to help mitigate drought stress during critical growing stages.

SCIENCE.head: Heat-Tolerant Mourvèdre Struggles as Southern France Gets Hotter
SCIENCE.summ: In a warming climate, even one of the world's most heat-tolerant grape varieties is reaching its limits
takeaways: Mourvèdre, historically suited to hot climates, now faces challenges maintaining balance and elegance as temperatures and drought intensify in southern France.
Producers in Bandol are experimenting with canopy management techniques like higher gobelet training and shade-providing plantings to protect grapes from excessive heat and sunburn.
Jumilla's vineyard area has halved in 20 years, with hotter southern sites abandoned in favor of higher-altitude parcels with better temperature moderation.
Bandol's rosé-dominated production may shift toward red wines as cooler sites traditionally reserved for rosé become necessary for heat-stressed vineyards.

SCIENCE.head: Organic Sauvignon Blanc challenging to grow but gaining traction
SCIENCE.summ: From fruity and tropical to stony and herbal, there's a Sauvignon style for everyone, if you know where to look. These organic examples are perfect for summer
takeaways: Sauvignon Blanc's sensitivity to powdery and downy mildew makes organic cultivation difficult, requiring frequent fungicide treatments.
Copper-based fungicides permitted for organic growers can damage the vine's flowers, creating a management dilemma.
Biodynamic farming builds resilient soil and helps overcome climate risks, according to practitioners in Pouilly-Fumé and Languedoc.
Organic Sauvignon Blancs range in style from tropical and rich to mineral and delicate depending on terroir and winemaking approach.
Nearly 20 years of organic farming can increase vineyard resilience to disease, particularly in favorable southern climates.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Highlights: Sandridge Barton East Field Pinot Noir Early 2025 Devon, England
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Another cracking lighter-style red from the UK! Sandridge Barton East Field Pinot Noir Early 2025 Devon, England11.2% alcohol. 100% Pinot
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jamie Goode
takeaways: Sandridge Barton East Field Pinot Noir Early 2025 from Devon, England: 11.2% alcohol, 100% Pinot Noir Precoce.
Production: 30% whole bunch, vatted 8 days, aged 8 months in stainless steel, no additions including sulfites.
Tasting profile: crunchy, vivid raspberry and cherry fruit with primary vibrancy and sweet texture; scored 93/100.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Wine News: What I’m Reading the Week of 7/12/26
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Hello and welcome to my weekly roundup of the wine stories that I find of interest on the web. I
NEWSLETTERS.author: Alder Yarrow

NEWSLETTERS.head: Portland, Maine: Come Hungry
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Hey, just a small catch-up after a few sweltering days at Pinewoods Dance Camp, where there was at least one day it was just too damned hot to leave Long Pond and balance and swing
NEWSLETTERS.author: Alice Feiring
takeaways: Alice Feiring recently visited Portland, Maine and recommends ordering cabbage dishes at restaurants there.
Feiring found every cabbage dish she tried in Portland to be excellent.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Mountains and Magliocco
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Calabria might be known as the land of wine, but it depends where you go
NEWSLETTERS.author: Simon J. Woolf
takeaways: Calabria has nine DOCs; only Cirò is widely known, despite vines grown there since before the Romans.
Magliocco Canino, possibly related to Sangiovese, retains acidity and ripens extremely late—Casa Comerci harvested their 2017 rosé in early December.
Casa Comerci, a 27-hectare certified organic estate near Nicotera, grows only Magliocco Canino and Greco Bianco, fermenting and aging exclusively in stainless steel.
Magliocco exists in two variants: the more common tannic Magliocco Dolce on Calabria's eastern seaboard, and Magliocco Canino on the western Tyrrhenian coast.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Too Difficult?
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Yeats entitled one of his mid-life poems ‘The Fascination of What’s Difficult’. The poem is mainly a complaint, about the tangled business of running a theatre (the Abbey in Dublin) and the infuriating difficulty of “management of men”, as opposed to the pure delight of writing poetry. But the title points to something which has
NEWSLETTERS.author: Tim Atkin MW
takeaways: Assyrtiko, Greece's assertive grape variety, divides drinkers with high acidity but rewards those who persevere.
Orange wines by Joska Gravner of Friuli initially lack traditional white wine qualities but reveal gravitas upon further tasting.
Nebbiolo-based reds of Piedmont combine pale color with high tannins, earth and smoke notes that challenge preconceptions based on French wines.
Sherry's deliberate oxidation and high acetaldehyde levels create initial resistance but deliver rewarding experiences for open-minded tasters.
